title: PhysX-Omni: Unified Simulation-Ready Physical 3D Generation for Rigid, Deformable, and Articulated Objects
summary: Simulation-ready physical 3D assets have emerged as a promising direction owing to their broad applicability in downstream tasks. However, most existing 3D generation methods either neglect physical properties or are limited to a single asset category, e.g., rigid, deformable, or articulated objects. To address these limitations, we introduce PhysX-Omni, a unified framework for simulation-ready physical 3D generation across diverse asset types. Specifically, we develop a novel and efficient geometry representation tailored for Vision-Language Models, which directly encodes high-resolution 3D structures without compression, significantly improving generation performance. In addition, we construct the first general simulation-ready 3D dataset, PhysXVerse, covering diverse indoor and outdoor categories. Furthermore, to comprehensively and flexibly evaluate both generative and understanding capabilities in the wild, we propose PhysX-Bench, which encompasses six key attributes: geometry, absolute scale, material, affordance, kinematics, and function description. Extensive experiments with conventional metrics and PhysX-Bench show that PhysX-Omni performs strongly in both generation and understanding. Moreover, additional studies further validate the potential of PhysX-Omni for applications in simulation-ready scene generation and robotic policy learning. We believe PhysX-Omni can significantly advance a wide range of downstream applications, particularly in embodied AI and physics-based simulation.

AI.tldr: PhysX-Omni is a unified framework for generating simulation-ready physical 3D assets across diverse categories (rigid, deformable, articulated objects), featuring a novel geometry representation for Vision-Language Models, a new dataset (PhysXVerse), and a comprehensive evaluation benchmark (PhysX-Bench).
AI.motivation: Existing 3D generation methods either neglect physical properties or are limited to a single asset category (rigid, deformable, or articulated objects), lacking a unified solution for simulation-ready physical 3D generation.
AI.method: (1) A novel geometry representation that directly encodes high-resolution 3D structures without compression for Vision-Language Models; (2) Construction of PhysXVerse, the first general simulation-ready 3D dataset; (3) PhysX-Bench for evaluating six attributes: geometry, absolute scale, material, affordance, kinematics, and function description.
AI.result: PhysX-Omni performs strongly in both generation and understanding tasks on conventional metrics and PhysX-Bench; validated potential for simulation-ready scene generation and robotic policy learning.
AI.conclusion: PhysX-Omni can significantly advance downstream applications, particularly in embodied AI and physics-based simulation.

title: Lens: Rethinking Training Efficiency for Foundational Text-to-Image Models
summary: We introduce Lens, a 3.8B-parameter T2I model that achieves performance competitive with, and in several cases surpassing, state-of-the-art models with more than 6B parameters across various benchmarks, while requiring significantly less training compute. For example, Lens requires only about 19.3% of the training compute used by Z-Image. The training efficiency of Lens stems from two key strategies beyond its compact model size. First, we maximize data information density per training batch by (i) training on Lens-800M, a dataset of 800M densely captioned image-text pairs whose captions are generated by GPT-4.1 and contain approximately 109 words on average, providing richer semantic supervision than conventional short captions, and (ii) constructing each batch from images with multiple resolutions and diverse aspect ratios, thereby enlarging the effective visual coverage of each optimization step. Second, we improve convergence speed through careful architectural choices, including adopting a semantic VAE that provides better latent representations and employing a strong language encoder that accelerates optimization while enabling multilingual generalization from English-only training data. After pre-training, we apply RL with taxonomy-driven prompts (Lens-RL-8K) and structured reward rubrics to suppress artifacts and improve visual quality, a reasoner module with training-free system prompt search to better align user requests with the model, and distillation-based acceleration for 4-step inference. Through efficient training and systematic optimization, Lens generalizes to arbitrary aspect ratios from 1:2 to 2:1 and resolutions up to 1440^2, and supports prompts in several commonly used languages. Thanks to its compact size, Lens generates a 1024^2 image in 3.15 seconds on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU, while its distilled turbo version performs 4-step generation in 0.84 seconds.

AI.tldr: Lens is a 3.8B-parameter T2I model that competes with 6B+ models using 19.3% of the training compute, achieved via dense captions (109-word avg), multi-resolution batching, semantic VAE, strong language encoder, RL fine-tuning, and distillation for 4-step inference.
AI.motivation: To build a high-quality text-to-image model that is computationally efficient during training while achieving state-of-the-art performance, challenging the trend of scaling model size without regard for efficiency.
AI.method: (1) Densely captioned dataset (Lens-800M, GPT-4.1 captions, ~109 words avg); (2) multi-resolution, diverse-aspect-ratio batching; (3) semantic VAE + strong language encoder; (4) RL with taxonomy-driven prompts and reward rubrics; (5) reasoner module with training-free system prompt search; (6) distillation for 4-step inference.
AI.result: Competitive or surpassing 6B+ models on benchmarks; 19.3% training compute of Z-Image; 1024² image in 3.15s (H100); distilled version in 0.84s (4-step); supports arbitrary aspect ratios 1:2~2:1, resolutions up to 1440², and multilingual prompts.
AI.conclusion: Compact model design combined with data efficiency, architectural choices, and post-training optimization can achieve or exceed the performance of much larger models at a fraction of the training cost.

title: GenEvolve: Self-Evolving Image Generation Agents via Tool-Orchestrated Visual Experience Distillation
summary: Open-ended image generation is no longer a simple prompt-to-image problem. High-quality generation often requires an agent to combine a model's internal generative ability with external resources. As requests become more diverse and demanding, we aim to develop a general image-generation agent that can self-evolve through trajectories and use tools more effectively across varied generation challenges. To this end, we propose GenEvolve, a self-evolving framework based on Tool-Orchestrated Visual Experience Distillation. In GenEvolve, each generation attempt is modeled as a tool-orchestrated trajectory, where the agent gathers evidence, selects references, invokes generation skills, and composes them into a prompt-reference program. Unlike existing agentic generation methods that mainly rely on image-level scalar rewards, GenEvolve compares multiple trajectories for the same request and abstracts best-worst differences into structured visual experience, provided only to a privileged teacher branch. Inspired by on-policy self-distillation, Visual Experience Distillation provides dense token-level supervision, helping the student internalize better search, knowledge activation, reference selection, and prompt construction. We further construct GenEvolve-Data and GenEvolve-Bench. Experiments on public benchmarks and GenEvolve-Bench show substantial gains over strong baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance among current image-generation frameworks. Our website is as follows: https://ephemeral182.github.io/GenEvolve/

AI.tldr: GenEvolve is a self-evolving framework for open-ended image generation that uses Tool-Orchestrated Visual Experience Distillation to improve an agent's ability to combine internal generative capabilities with external tools through trajectory learning and dense token-level supervision.
AI.motivation: Open-ended image generation requires more than simple prompt-to-image mapping; there is a need for agents that can self-evolve and effectively use external tools across diverse and demanding generation requests.
AI.method: GenEvolve models each generation attempt as a tool-orchestrated trajectory, compares multiple trajectories for the same request, abstracts best-worst differences into structured visual experience via a privileged teacher branch, and uses Visual Experience Distillation (inspired by on-policy self-distillation) to provide dense token-level supervision for the student model.
AI.result: Achieves substantial gains over strong baselines and state-of-the-art performance on public benchmarks and the authors' constructed GenEvolve-Bench.
AI.conclusion: GenEvolve demonstrates that self-evolving through trajectory comparison and token-level distillation significantly improves tool-use effectiveness and generation quality in open-ended image generation tasks.

title: UniVL: Unified Vision-Language Embedding for Spatially Grounded Contextual Image Generation
summary:
We introduce spatially grounded contextual image generation, a controllable image generation task that reframes the conditioning paradigm. Instead of supplying a reference image and a global text prompt through two separate encoders, one for vision and one for language, UniVL is trained to bind semantics to spatial locations directly from a single unified visual input, where the textual instruction is rendered onto the spatial mask. This removes the need for a standalone text encoder at inference time. The resulting model supports contextual image generation by following user-specified instructions about what should appear where, while substantially reducing computation.
  To address this task, we propose a framework in which the UniVL encoder, adapted from an optical-character-recognition-pretrained backbone, reads the unified condition optically and produces a UniVL embedding, fVIL, that fuses visual and semantic intent with spatial locations in a single token sequence. A two-stage pipeline first aligns UniVL with the VAE embedding space and then conditions a pretrained diffusion backbone entirely on UniVL embeddings, eliminating the standalone text encoder, such as T5. Although this reframing uses a deliberately minimal text interface, it yields strong empirical gains. On UniVL-ImgGen, a benchmark of 477K mask-annotated images that we construct for training and evaluation, UniVL improves image quality over text-prompted baselines, reducing FID from 14 to 11 and increasing PSNR from 16 to 20. It also eliminates the text encoder entirely, reducing inference TFLOPs by up to 52% and runtime by up to 44%. Additional ablation studies validate the contributions of the proposed components, paving the way for efficient, spatially grounded image generation with a unified conditioning paradigm.

AI.tldr: UniVL is a spatially grounded image generation model that replaces separate text and image encoders with a unified visual input containing rendered text instructions, achieving better quality (FID 14→11, PSNR 16→20) while reducing inference TFLOPs by 52% and runtime by 44%.
AI.motivation: Existing contextual image generation relies on two separate encoders (vision + language/global text prompt), which is computationally expensive and complex. The authors aim to simplify this by binding semantics to spatial locations directly from a single unified visual input.
AI.method: UniVL uses an OCR-pretrained backbone to optically read a unified input (text rendered onto spatial mask), producing fVIL embeddings that fuse visual/semantic intent with spatial locations. A two-stage pipeline aligns with VAE space and conditions a pretrained diffusion backbone, eliminating the standalone text encoder (e.g., T5).
AI.result: On the UniVL-ImgGen benchmark (477K mask-annotated images), UniVL improves FID from 14→11 and PSNR from 16→20, reduces inference TFLOPs by up to 52% and runtime by up to 44%.
AI.conclusion: UniVL demonstrates that unified conditioning (text rendered on spatial masks) can outperform two-encoder baselines while being more efficient, paving the way for spatially grounded image generation without a standalone text encoder.

title: Flying Together: Human-Guided Immersive Shared Control for Aerial Robot Teams in Unknown Environments
comment: Accepted at IEEE International Conference in Robotics and Automation, Vienna 2026
summary: While autonomous multi-robots can achieve safe and coordinated navigation, they often struggle to adapt to unforeseen conditions and to capture operator-driven objectives in unstructured environments. We present a Virtual Reality (VR)-based shared control framework for teams of drones operating in constrained and unknown environments, enabling real-time, user-guided exploration. At the core of our approach is a novel, user-guided motion-primitive-based planner that computes continuous, collision-free trajectories while continuously integrating operator input. This planner is coupled with an admittance controller, allowing the operator to flexibly influence team behavior and guide drones toward regions of interest that autonomous planners may overlook. The system supports mixed-reality operations with both physical and simulated drones, and implements a bilateral VR-based interface, allowing the operator to guide the robot team via migration points while receiving immediate visual feedback of the team state. Experimental results show that shared control improves obstacle avoidance, maintains inter-agent spacing, and reduces operator effort, demonstrating the feasibility and advantages of immersive, human-in-the-loop multi-robot navigation.

AI.tldr: A VR-based shared control framework for drone teams that combines user-guided motion planning with admittance control, enabling operators to guide multi-robot navigation in constrained environments while reducing effort and improving safety.
AI.motivation: Autonomous multi-robot systems struggle to adapt to unforeseen conditions and capture operator-driven objectives in unstructured environments, highlighting the need for human-in-the-loop control.
AI.method: VR-based shared control with a user-guided motion-primitive planner that computes collision-free trajectories while integrating operator input, coupled with an admittance controller and bilateral VR interface for guidance via migration points.
AI.result: Shared control improves obstacle avoidance, maintains inter-agent spacing, and reduces operator effort compared to fully autonomous navigation.
AI.conclusion: Immersive human-in-the-loop control via VR is feasible and advantageous for multi-robot navigation in constrained, unknown environments.

title: Benchmarking and Improving Monitors for Out-Of-Distribution Alignment Failure in LLMs
summary: Many safety and alignment failures of large language models (LLMs) occur due to out-of-distribution (OOD) situations: unusual prompt or response patterns that are unforeseen by model developers. We systematically study whether LLM monitoring pipelines can detect these OOD alignment failures by introducing a benchmark called Misalignment Out Of Distribution (MOOD). It is difficult to find failures that are truly OOD for off-the-shelf models trained on vast safety datasets. We sidestep this by including a restricted training set in MOOD that we use to train our own monitors, as well as seven test sets with diverse alignment failures that are outside the training distribution. Using MOOD, we find that guard models (safety classifiers) often fail to generalize OOD. To fix this, we propose combining guard models with OOD detectors. We test four types of OOD detectors and find that a combination of a guard model with Mahalanobis distance and perplexity-based OOD detectors can improve recall from 39% to 45%. We also establish positive scaling trends across model scales for monitors that combine a guard model and OOD detector; we find that incorporating OOD detection into monitoring achieves a higher recall gain than using a guard model with 20 times more parameters. Our work suggests that OOD detection should be a crucial component of LLM monitoring and provides a foundation for further work on this important problem.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MOOD, a benchmark for detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) alignment failures in LLMs, and shows that combining guard models with OOD detectors (Mahalanobis distance and perplexity) improves failure detection recall from 39% to 45%, with gains exceeding those from simply scaling up model size.
AI.motivation: Many LLM safety failures occur due to out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios that model developers cannot anticipate, yet existing monitoring pipelines struggle to detect these OOD alignment failures.
AI.method: The authors introduce the MOOD benchmark with a restricted training set and seven OOD test sets. They train guard models (safety classifiers) and combine them with four types of OOD detectors (Mahalanobis distance, perplexity, etc.), evaluating recall improvement.
AI.result: Guard models alone fail to generalize OOD. Combining a guard model with Mahalanobis distance and perplexity-based OOD detectors improves recall from 39% to 45%. This OOD detection gain surpasses using a guard model with 20x more parameters.
AI.conclusion: OOD detection should be a crucial component of LLM monitoring pipelines, offering a more effective path to safety than simply scaling model size.

title: Flat-Pack Bench: Evaluating Spatio-Temporal Understanding in Large Vision-Language Models through Furniture Assembly
summary: The emergence of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) has significantly advanced video understanding capabilities. However, existing benchmarks focus predominantly on coarse-grained tasks such as action segmentation, classification, captioning, and retrieval. Furthermore, these benchmarks often rely on entities that can be easily identified verbally, like household objects, animals, human subjects, etc., limiting their applicability to complex, in-the-wild video scenarios. But, many applications such as furniture assembly, cooking, etc., require step-by-step fine-grained spatio-temporal understanding of the video, which is not sufficiently evaluated in current benchmarks. To address this gap, we introduce Flat-Pack Bench, a novel benchmark centered on furniture assembly tasks. Our benchmark evaluates LVLMs on nuanced tasks, including temporal ordering of assembly actions, temporal localization of assembly state, understanding part mating, and tracking, using multiple-choice questions paired with visual prompts highlighting relevant parts as references for fine-grained questions. Our experiments reveal that state-of-the-art LVLMs struggle significantly with fine-grained spatio-temporal reasoning, highlighting their limitations in effectively leveraging temporal information from videos, limited tracking ability, and understanding of spatial interactions like physical contact.

AI.tldr: Flat-Pack Bench is a new benchmark evaluating LVLMs on fine-grained spatio-temporal video understanding through furniture assembly tasks, revealing that current models struggle with temporal reasoning, tracking, and spatial interaction understanding.
AI.motivation: Existing video understanding benchmarks focus on coarse-grained tasks (action segmentation, classification, captioning, retrieval) and rely on easily identifiable entities, failing to evaluate fine-grained spatio-temporal reasoning needed for complex real-world applications like furniture assembly and cooking.
AI.method: The authors introduce Flat-Pack Bench, a benchmark centered on furniture assembly tasks, with multiple-choice questions covering temporal ordering of assembly actions, temporal localization of assembly state, part mating understanding, and tracking, using visual prompts to highlight relevant parts.
AI.result: State-of-the-art LVLMs perform poorly on Flat-Pack Bench, struggling significantly with fine-grained spatio-temporal reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Current LVLMs have fundamental limitations in leveraging temporal video information, tracking objects, and understanding spatial interactions like physical contact, highlighting the need for improved fine-grained video understanding capabilities.

title: Distributed Multi-Coverage for Robot Swarms
comment: Accepted at ANTS 2026 (International Conference on Swarm Intelligence), published by Springer Nature
summary: Autonomous drone swarms deployed for surveillance, environmental monitoring, and infrastructure inspection must maintain reliable coverage of critical assets despite robot failures. This requires multicoverage: each asset must be observed by multiple robots for redundancy, with coverage requirements varying by asset importance. While recent work has solved the centralized problem optimally using integer programming, practical deployments face constraints that demand distributed solutions: robots operate with limited communication ranges, onboard computation restricts global planning, and partial system failures must not cause mission abort. We present a distributed multicoverage algorithm for robot swarms operating with local sensing, local communication, and no global coordination.

AI.tldr: A distributed multicoverage algorithm for robot swarms that maintains reliable asset coverage despite failures, using only local sensing and communication.
AI.motivation: Autonomous drone swarms need reliable multicoverage of critical assets despite robot failures, but existing optimal solutions are centralized and impractical for real deployments with limited communication, onboard computation, and partial system failures.
AI.method: A fully distributed multicoverage algorithm that operates with local sensing, local communication, and no global coordination.
AI.result: Enables robot swarms to maintain varying multicoverage requirements for assets without centralized planning.
AI.conclusion: Distributed multicoverage is feasible for practical drone swarm deployments under realistic constraints of limited communication and computation.

title: TO-Agents: A Multi-Agent AI Pipeline for Preference-Guided Topology Optimization
comment: Accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the ASME 2026 International Design Engineering Technical Conferences (IDETC2026)
summary: Topology optimization can generate efficient structures, but designers often must manually translate qualitative intent, such as desired visual style, product experience, or manufacturability into solver settings that are not directly tied to those preferences. We present TO-Agents, a multi-agent AI framework that connects natural-language design intent with iterative topology optimization. The framework converts a human-provided problem description into validated solver inputs, runs a topology optimization solver, renders the resulting 3D topology, and uses multi-view vision-language reasoning with an independent judge agent to critique each result and revise solver parameters. We evaluate the framework on two long-horizon design tasks: a cantilever beam benchmark and a phone-stand product design. In both tasks, the designer specifies an aesthetic preference for hierarchically branched structures inspired by natural tree morphologies, and the system performs four revision cycles across ten independent replicates. TO-Agents produces at least one preference-aligned design in 60% of trials for each case study, corresponding to up to 6x more successful trials than an ablated pipeline without visual or historical feedback. Judge scores and human evaluations show that the pipeline can identify effective parameter levers, recover from poor revisions, and expand design exploration. A manufacturing agent further post-processes top-ranked designs for additive manufacturing, enabling end-to-end intent-to-prototype design. We also identify failure modes, including overshooting, selective memory, misplaced tools, and incorrect parameter reasoning. These results suggest that agentic topology optimization can shift designers from low-level parameter tuning toward higher-level specification of form and function, while highlighting safeguards needed for reliable autonomous engineering design.

AI.tldr: TO-Agents is a multi-agent AI framework that connects natural-language design intent with iterative topology optimization, using vision-language reasoning and an independent judge agent to critique and revise designs. It achieves preference-aligned designs in 60% of trials across cantilever beam and phone-stand tasks, with up to 6x improvement over ablated baselines, enabling end-to-end intent-to-prototype design.
AI.motivation: Designers must manually translate qualitative preferences (style, experience, manufacturability) into solver parameters that are not directly tied to those preferences, creating a gap between human intent and optimization outcomes.
AI.method: A multi-agent framework that converts natural-language descriptions → validated solver inputs → runs topology optimization → renders 3D topology → uses multi-view vision-language reasoning with an independent judge agent to critique results and revise parameters iteratively. A manufacturing agent post-processes top designs for 3D printing.
AI.result: 60% of trials produce preference-aligned designs (hierarchically branched structures) across both case studies, up to 6x more than ablation without visual/historical feedback. Judge scores and human evaluations confirm effective parameter levers, recovery from poor revisions, and expanded design exploration.
AI.conclusion: Agentic topology optimization can shift designers from low-level parameter tuning toward high-level specification of form and function, but safeguards are needed for reliable autonomous engineering design.

title: Ablate-to-Validate: Are Vision-Language Models Really Using Continuous Thought Tokens?
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly augmented with continuous or latent non-textual tokens intended to support "visual thinking." Despite improved task accuracy, this alone does not show that models actually use these tokens for reasoning -- gains may arise from confounds such as added context length, special-token anchoring, or training-time regularization. We formalize a diagnostic principle, Ablate-to-Validate, for testing whether latent-token content is genuinely utilized, and instantiate it as the Token Replacement Test (TRT), a standardized suite of content-replacement ablations. TRT holds the prompt, image, token budget, and decoding fixed while replacing intermediate tokens with zero, random, first-repeat, or oracle alternatives, isolating whether performance depends on token content or merely on token presence. As a controlled testbed, we study relative depth reasoning with LLaVA-13B and Qwen2.5-VL-3B, training models to predict and consume continuous or discrete depth spans across multiple frozen encoders (SigLIP2, CLIP, DINOv2) and token budgets. We additionally apply TRT to three off-the-shelf visual-thinking systems (Mirage, Mull-Tokens, CoVT) on BLINK, VSP, and CV-Bench. Across all settings, accuracy gains are a misleading proxy for latent-token reasoning: VLMs retain most improvement even when token content is corrupted or replaced, revealing a persistent gap between having a latent channel and using it as an information bottleneck. We recommend TRT as a standard diagnostic alongside accuracy for any method introducing continuous thought tokens.

AI.tldr: Vision-language models show accuracy gains from latent tokens, but these gains persist even when token content is corrupted, indicating the tokens aren't actually used for reasoning—just their presence matters.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations of latent/continuous tokens in VLMs only measure task accuracy, which is insufficient to prove the tokens are genuinely used for reasoning; gains may come from confounds like added context length or regularization.
AI.method: The authors propose Ablate-to-Validate, instantiated as the Token Replacement Test (TRT), which replaces intermediate latent tokens with zero, random, first-repeat, or oracle alternatives while keeping prompt, image, token budget, and decoding fixed. They study LLaVA-13B and Qwen2.5-VL-3B on depth reasoning, and also test off-the-shelf systems (Mirage, Mull-Tokens, CoVT).
AI.result: Across all settings, accuracy gains persist even when token content is corrupted or replaced, showing that VLMs retain most improvement without genuinely using the tokens for reasoning—revealing a gap between having a latent channel and using it as an information bottleneck.
AI.conclusion: Accuracy alone is a misleading proxy for latent-token reasoning; TRT should be used as a standard diagnostic alongside accuracy for any method introducing continuous thought tokens.

title: Closed-Loop Sim-to-Real Reinforcement Learning for Deformable Microfiber Shape Control
summary: Autonomous contact-based micromanipulation is challenging because surface and interfacial interactions at the microscale are difficult to model accurately, limiting the use of conventional model-based control and sim-to-real learning. We present a closed-loop sim-to-real reinforcement learning (RL) approach for microfiber shape control on a surface. The central idea is to train geometric shape regulation in a simplified frictionless simulator and rely on real-time visual feedback during deployment to iteratively correct the observed effects of unmodeled surface interactions. An RL policy trained entirely in simulation is transferred directly to a physical dual-gripper micromanipulation system operating at 40 Hz, without retraining or domain adaptation. Using silk microfibers as a testbed, the policy achieves a mean point-wise shape error of 270 $\pm$ 80 $μ$m across twenty-four diverse initial configurations. Across nine specimens covering all combinations of three fiber diameters (50, 80, and 120 $μ$m) and three manipulated lengths (10 mm, 15mm, and 20 mm), the same policy achieves sub-millimeter final shape error without any retraining or retuning. These results show that a policy learned in a simplified simulator can achieve repeatable real-world microfiber shape regulation under surface contact, provided that the task-relevant effects of the sim-to-real mismatch remain observable and correctable within the closed feedback loop.

AI.tldr: A closed-loop sim-to-real RL approach achieves microfiber shape control by training in a simplified frictionless simulator and using real-time visual feedback to correct unmodeled surface interactions, achieving sub-millimeter accuracy across diverse configurations without retraining.
AI.motivation: Autonomous contact-based micromanipulation is challenging because microscale surface/interfacial interactions are difficult to model accurately, limiting model-based control and sim-to-real learning.
AI.method: Training an RL policy in a simplified frictionless simulator, then directly deploying it on a physical dual-gripper system at 40 Hz using real-time visual feedback to iteratively correct unmodeled surface interactions, without retraining or domain adaptation.
AI.result: Mean point-wise shape error of 270±80 μm across 24 initial configurations; sub-millimeter error across 9 specimens (3 diameters × 3 lengths) without retraining or retuning.
AI.conclusion: A policy learned in a simplified simulator can achieve repeatable real-world microfiber shape regulation under surface contact, as long as sim-to-real mismatches remain observable and correctable within the closed feedback loop.

title: The Shape of Testimony: A Scalable Framework for Oral History Archive Comparison
summary: Researchers in Holocaust studies have often distinguished between two styles of oral survivor testimony: the USC Shoah Foundation's interviews tend to follow a structured, interviewer-guided format, whereas the Yale Fortunoff Video Archive generally favors a more free-form, open-ended style. This distinction has influenced both scholarly research and the development of later archives. In this study, we critically examine that claim by conducting a large-scale computational analysis of more than 1,600 testimonies from both collections. Leveraging discourse segmentation, topic modeling, and large language model (LLM) based analysis, we quantify the "structuredness" level of testimonies through topic coherence, interviewer-survivor dynamics, and the distribution of question types. Our results generally corroborate the structural differences identified in earlier research, while also revealing significant overlaps between the collections, both within individual interviews and across common narrative patterns. This complicates the simple "structured vs. free-form" dichotomy often applied to these oral histories. Beyond revisiting a foundational claim in Holocaust studies, our work provides a scalable, replicable framework for comparative corpus analysis. As a proof of concept, it suggests broader applications for digital oral history, narrative analysis, and the design of citizen-science annotation platforms.

AI.tldr: A large-scale computational analysis of over 1,600 Holocaust testimonies from USC Shoah Foundation and Yale Fortunoff Archive reveals that while structural differences exist, the rigid "structured vs. free-form" dichotomy oversimplifies the reality, as significant overlaps complicate this distinction.
AI.motivation: To critically examine the widely accepted claim that USC Shoah Foundation interviews follow a structured format while Yale Fortunoff Archive favors open-ended styles—a distinction influencing Holocaust studies and archive development.
AI.method: Applied computational analysis (discourse segmentation, topic modeling, LLM-based analysis) to quantify "structuredness" via topic coherence, interviewer-survivor dynamics, and question type distribution across 1,600+ testimonies from both archives.
AI.result: Corroborated existing structural differences but discovered significant overlaps between collections—both within individual interviews and across narrative patterns—complicating the simple dichotomy.
AI.conclusion: The "structured vs. free-form" binary is an oversimplification; computational methods provide a scalable, replicable framework for comparative oral history analysis with broader applications in digital humanities.

title: Look-Closer-Then-Diagnose: Confidence-Aware Ultrasound VQA via Active Zooming
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have significantly advanced medical visual question answering, yet their performance in ultrasound remains suboptimal. In clinical practice, sonographers explicitly focus on lesion regions to formulate reports, though diagnostic interpretations sometimes vary due to inherent subjectivity. However, existing VLMs are not explicitly structured to interactively zoom into lesions prior to diagnosis; moreover, they typically treat annotations as unbiased ground truths, failing to account for their inherent subjectivity and ambiguity. In this paper, we propose a framework specifically designed to consider the sonographer's cognitive workflow. We first introduce a structured Zoom-then-Diagnose paradigm, which replicates the interactive search process to enable lesion-focused reasoning. Furthermore, within the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) framework, we introduce an uncertainty-aware reward derived from stochastic group-wise rollouts to estimate prediction consistency as a proxy for model confidence. Together, these two components encourage the model to reinforce accurate predictions on clear cases while remaining cautious under ambiguity. Experiments across liver, breast, and thyroid datasets show that our framework improves lesion localization by 39.3\%, demonstrating that our model has learned the ability to actively look closer and diagnose.

AI.tldr: Proposes Zoom-then-Diagnose + uncertainty-aware GRPO framework that improves VLM performance on ultrasound by mimicking sonographers' lesion-focused workflow and handling annotation subjectivity.
AI.motivation: Existing VLMs fail to explicitly zoom into lesion regions before diagnosis and treat all annotations as unbiased ground truths, ignoring the inherent subjectivity and ambiguity in ultrasound interpretation.
AI.method: (1) Zoom-then-Diagnose paradigm that replicates interactive search for lesion-focused reasoning; (2) Uncertainty-aware reward within GRPO using stochastic group-wise rollouts to estimate confidence, encouraging caution under ambiguity.
AI.result: Lesion localization improved by 39.3% across liver, breast, and thyroid ultrasound datasets.
AI.conclusion: The model learns to actively "look closer and diagnose," aligning with sonographers' cognitive workflow and handling ambiguous cases more effectively.

title: Motion Design for Grasp-Based Dynamic Locomotion in Microgravity
summary: Locomotion in microgravity often relies on sparsely and irregularly arranged anchors, motivating grasp-based mobility with multiple limbs. In this setting, dynamic locomotion is feasible only through deliberate regulation of both anchored interactions and whole-body coordination under coupled dynamic and kinematic constraints. This paper presents design insights for grasp-based dynamic locomotion with multi-limbed robotic systems in microgravity, targeting scenarios that require 6D limb manipulation to establish contacts with candidate anchors. The investigated design parameters include gait pattern, stride length, locomotion speed, and nominal posture. A parameterizable locomotion planning framework is proposed to support variations of these parameters and to evaluate the resulting locomotion performance in terms of stability and actuation demand. Two representative quadruped morphologies are adopted for evaluation in physics-based simulation. The results demonstrate that enlarging the feasible contact wrench space and attenuating impulsive whole-body dynamics improve locomotion performance. These findings inform strategies for contact configuration selection and whole-body coordination in microgravity locomotion with multi-limbed systems.

AI.tldr: This paper studies how multi-limbed robots can perform grasp-based dynamic locomotion in microgravity, proposing a parameterizable planning framework and evaluating it on two quadruped morphologies to identify design insights for improved stability and reduced actuation demand.
AI.motivation: Locomotion in microgravity relies on sparse, irregular anchors, and dynamic movement requires careful coordination of anchored interactions and whole-body motion under coupled dynamic/kinematic constraints.
AI.method: A parameterizable locomotion planning framework is proposed, varying gait pattern, stride length, speed, and nominal posture. Two quadruped morphologies are evaluated via physics-based simulation.
AI.result: Enlarging the feasible contact wrench space and attenuating impulsive whole-body dynamics improve locomotion performance in microgravity.
AI.conclusion: Findings inform strategies for contact configuration selection and whole-body coordination in microgravity locomotion with multi-limbed systems.

title: MindLoom: Composing Thought Modes for Frontier-Level Reasoning Data Synthesis
comment: Work in Progress. Comments: 27 pages, 4 figures, preprint
summary: Although LLMs have made substantial progress in reasoning, systematically producing frontier-level reasoning data remains difficult. Existing synthesis methods often have limited visibility into the structural factors that govern problem difficulty, which can result in narrow diversity and unstable difficulty control. In this work, we view the difficulty of a reasoning problem as arising from the accumulation of atomic knowledge-reasoning transformations, which we term thought modes. Building on this perspective, we propose MindLoom, a framework for synthesizing frontier-level reasoning data through compositional thought mode engineering. Given a collection of hard problems with verified solutions, MindLoom first decomposes those solutions into thought mode chains that reveal each problem's construction logic. It then trains a retrieval model that matches problem states to compatible thought modes, providing guidance on which reasoning challenges to introduce during synthesis. New problems are composed by iteratively applying retrieved thought modes to seed questions, with distribution-aligned sampling to encourage diverse reasoning coverage. Finally, a rollout-based judging stage labels generated questions by difficulty and supplies judged-correct responses for supervised fine-tuning. We evaluate MindLoom on nine benchmarks covering five STEM disciplines and four mathematical reasoning tasks across multiple model families and sizes. Models fine-tuned on MindLoom-generated data achieves favorable performances over base models, distillation, and external-data baselines across the reported benchmarks. Ablation studies indicate the contribution of each component, and further analysis suggests that MindLoom covers a broad range of reasoning patterns while maintaining useful difficulty control. We have open-sourced our implementation at https://github.com/EachSheep/MindLoom.

AI.tldr: MindLoom is a framework that synthesizes frontier-level reasoning data for LLMs by decomposing hard problems into "thought mode chains" (atomic knowledge-reasoning transformations), then using retrieval-based composition and rollout judging to generate diverse, difficulty-controlled training data. It achieves strong results across 9 STEM/math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing reasoning data synthesis methods lack visibility into structural factors governing problem difficulty, leading to narrow diversity and unstable difficulty control.
AI.method: 1) Decompose hard problem solutions into thought mode chains; 2) Train a retrieval model matching problem states to compatible thought modes; 3) Compose new problems by iteratively applying retrieved thought modes to seed questions with distribution-aligned sampling; 4) Use rollout-based judging to label difficulty and supply correct responses for SFT.
AI.result: Models fine-tuned on MindLoom data outperform base models, distillation, and external-data baselines across 9 benchmarks (5 STEM disciplines, 4 math reasoning tasks). Ablations confirm each component's contribution.
AI.conclusion: MindLoom provides effective difficulty control and broad reasoning pattern coverage for synthesizing high-quality reasoning data.

title: MRecover: A Conditional Generative Model for Recovering Motion-Corrupted MR images Using AI Generated Contrast
summary: Hippocampal subfield segmentation requires high-resolution T2w turbo spin echo (TSE) MRI, yet this sequence is susceptible to motion artifacts, leading to substantial data loss. We developed a conditional generative model (MRecover) that synthesizes routinely acquired T1w images to create TSE images with autoregressive slice conditioning for volumetric consistency. Trained on 7T MRI data (n=577), the model achieved high in-domain fidelity (n=148, SSIM=0.84, FSIM=0.94) and generalized well to out-of-domain 3T data: subfield volumes from synthesized and the as-acquired images closely matched: (n=416, r=0.87-0.97) and yielded 31.8% more analyzable subjects in the motion-affected ADNI3 dataset after quality control (593 vs 450). The synthesized images also achieved larger effect sizes due to increasing the sample size for diagnostic group differences in hippocampal subfield atrophy (whole hippocampus $ε^2$= 0.121-0.100 vs. 0.086-0.062, left-right hemispheres). Project page: https://jinghangli98.github.io/MRecover/

AI.tldr: MRecover is a conditional generative model that synthesizes TSE-like MRI images from T1w images to recover motion-corrupted hippocampal subfield segmentation data, improving analyzable subject yield by 31.8% and increasing statistical power for detecting atrophy differences.
AI.motivation: Hippocampal subfield segmentation requires high-resolution T2w TSE MRI, which is highly susceptible to motion artifacts leading to substantial data loss.
AI.method: A conditional generative model (MRecover) that synthesizes T1w images into TSE images using autoregressive slice conditioning for volumetric consistency, trained on 7T MRI data (n=577).
AI.result: High in-domain fidelity (SSIM=0.84, FSIM=0.94), excellent out-of-domain generalization to 3T (r=0.87-0.97), 31.8% more analyzable subjects (593 vs 450), and larger effect sizes for hippocampal subfield atrophy detection.
AI.conclusion: MRecover effectively recovers motion-corrupted TSE MRI data, improving sample utility and statistical power for hippocampal subfield analysis.

title: PGDG: Physically Grounded Data Generation for Robust Bimanual Policy Learning from a Single Demonstration
summary: Behavior cloning for contact-rich bimanual manipulation remains challenging because diverse demonstrations are expensive to collect, and even small disturbances can push the system into off-manifold states where no recovery supervision is available. We propose PGDG, a data generation framework with zero-shot curation that expands a single demonstration into a compact dataset of physically plausible, successful, and diverse recovery behaviors without additional human labeling. PGDG iterates between a physics-grounded sampler and a dataset curator, where the curator selects informative, non-redundant, and recoverable behaviors to update the sampling distribution toward under-covered recovery modes, and the sampler draws physically plausible rollout candidates from this updated distribution and retains successful trajectories. To further improve data quality, PGDG applies short-horizon sampling-based control to relabel selected risky states with corrective actions. Across four bimanual manipulation tasks, PGDG consistently outperforms spatial-only augmentation in both simulation and zero-shot real-world transfer. On RotateBox-Pitch, success improves from 38% to 93% in simulation and from 35% to 82% in the real world. PGDG also enables effective foundation models fine-tuning such as GR00T, increasing success from 46% to 77%. Additional results are available in our website: https://cunxid.github.io/PGDG/.

AI.tldr: PGDG is a zero-shot data generation framework that expands a single demonstration into a diverse dataset of recovery behaviors for contact-rich bimanual manipulation, significantly improving success rates in both simulation and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Behavior cloning for bimanual manipulation fails due to costly diverse demonstrations and lack of recovery supervision under disturbances.
AI.method: PGDG iterates between a physics-grounded sampler and a dataset curator to select informative behaviors, then applies short-horizon sampling-based control to relabel risky states.
AI.result: Outperforms spatial-only augmentation; RotateBox-Pitch success from 38%→93% in simulation, 35%→82% in real world; GR00T fine-tuning from 46%→77%.
AI.conclusion: PGDG enables zero-shot curation of diverse, physically plausible recovery behaviors from a single demonstration, improving BC robustness.

title: AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0: Data Model Expansions and Content Evaluation Framework for Using Agentic AI to Improve Integration between AOPs and New Approach Methodologies (NAMs)
summary: Adverse Outcome Pathways (AOP) are logic models that causally link biological mechanisms that can be measured in a lab to adverse outcomes, relevant to chemical regulatory endpoints. AOPs contextualize new approach methodologies (NAMs), in vitro and in silico methods used as alternatives to animal testing and the sequential events in an AOP serve as multi-scale models spanning biological scales. The AOP-Wiki serves as the global repository for AOPs. While the AOP-Wiki has played a central role in AOP expansion over the past decade, constraints within the current data model and application infrastructure limit the AOP-Wiki from supporting continued AOP growth and evolution. Yet, the transformative power of agentic AI has re-invigorated AOP-Wiki data modernization efforts at a time when core AOP principles can be harnessed to inform use of AI for aggregating and structuring AOP-relevant information. Seizing upon this momentum, we present AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0, the third in a series of evidence model prototypes, which concretely demonstrates data model expansions and our vision for how the AOP-Wiki might be transformed to better serve regulatory science and emergent use of AOPs in biomedical and One Health contexts. We aim to lay a foundation to support computationally-generated AOPs and quantitative AOPs (qAOPs) by focussing on solutions for AOP-Wiki internal quality improvement, evidence structuring to enhance AOP FAIRness and AI-readiness, and improved integration between the AOP framework and NAMs to better serve next generation risk assessment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0, a prototype demonstrating data model expansions to modernize the AOP-Wiki repository, enabling support for computationally-generated AOPs, quantitative AOPs, improved FAIRness, AI-readiness, and integration with new approach methodologies for next-generation risk assessment.
AI.motivation: Current AOP-Wiki data model and infrastructure constraints limit continued AOP growth despite its central role; the emergence of agentic AI provides renewed momentum for modernization to better serve regulatory science and broader biomedical/One Health applications.
AI.method: Development of AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0, the third evidence model prototype, focusing on data model expansions, internal quality improvement, evidence structuring for FAIRness and AI-readiness, and improved AOP-NAM integration.
AI.result: A concrete demonstration of how the AOP-Wiki can be transformed to support computationally-generated AOPs, quantitative AOPs (qAOPs), and next-generation risk assessment.
AI.conclusion: Lays a foundation for transforming AOP-Wiki to better serve regulatory science, biomedical, and One Health contexts through enhanced data models that leverage AI and improve interoperability.

title: AVI-HT: Adaptive Vision-IMU Fusion for 3D Hand Tracking
summary: We present AVI-HT, an adaptive visual-IMU fusion approach for tracking 3D hand poses by jointly modeling the egocentric image with on-glove 6-DoF IMU signals. AVI-HT achieves significantly improved accuracy and availability, particularly in hand-object interaction (HOI) scenarios involving heavy visual occlusion. Two complementary ingredients underpin its success: (1) synchronized multi-modal training data pairing on-body vision-IMU sensor streams with ground-truth 3D hand poses from a motion-capture system, and (2) a cross-sensor deep attention mechanism that adaptively modulates the trust assigned to the vision and individual IMU sensors. To evaluate AVI-HT in real-world settings, we conduct extensive experiments on our DexGloveHOI dataset that consists of 100K+ pairwise vision-IMU samples with synchronized 3D annotated poses, in which users manipulate a variety of objects during daily tasks. We compare against multiple single- and multi-modal tracking approaches under two hand models (UmeTrack, MANO). The results show that AVI-HT reduces mean keypoint error by 16.1% and its wrist-aligned variant by 24.2% over the baselines. Ablation studies further reveal the per-finger contribution of IMU sensors across activity types, and the model's sensitivity to IMU noise and temporal misalignment in vision-IMU fusion.

AI.tldr: AVI-HT is an adaptive visual-IMU fusion method for 3D hand tracking that uses cross-sensor attention to handle visual occlusion during hand-object interactions, reducing keypoint error by 16-24% over baselines.
AI.motivation: Accurate 3D hand tracking during hand-object interaction (HOI) is challenging due to heavy visual occlusion, which degrades vision-only approaches. The authors aim to improve tracking robustness by fusing egocentric vision with on-glove IMU signals through adaptive sensor fusion.
AI.method: AVI-HT jointly models egocentric images with 6-DoF IMU signals from on-glove sensors, using synchronized multi-modal training data (100K+ samples from DexGloveHOI dataset) and a cross-sensor deep attention mechanism that adaptively weights vision and individual IMU sensors based on trust.
AI.result: Reduces mean keypoint error by 16.1% (24.2% for wrist-aligned variant) over multiple single/multi-modal baselines on two hand models (UmeTrack, MANO). Ablation studies reveal per-finger IMU contributions and sensitivity to noise/temporal misalignment.
AI.conclusion: AVI-HT significantly improves 3D hand tracking accuracy and availability under occlusion-heavy HOI scenarios, with adaptive attention being key to robust multi-modal fusion.

title: Mind the Gaps: Multi-Robot Feedback-Driven Ergodic Coverage in Unknown Environments
summary: In this work, we address the problem of multi-robot adaptive coverage, where teams of robots perform dynamic sampling by continuously adjusting their positions to collect data in an environment. This task can be challenging, particularly when robots must be efficiently allocated to new sampling locations over time. Ergodic search methods optimize robot trajectories by ensuring that the robots' time-averaged spatial distribution aligns with the spatial distribution of environmental information. While these methods promote effective exploration provided a target distribution, they often fail to account for unknown prior distributions of the environment. To overcome this limitation, we propose an adaptive coverage strategy that utilizes real-time feedback from an environmental model to adjust robot sampling behavior in response to unknown conditions. Our approach enhances traditional ergodic trajectory optimization by constructing a target spatial information distribution based on parametric models of the environment, which are updated online. This strategy assumes that the environment is either static or changes slowly compared to the robot's motion. Our framework allows robots to dynamically prioritize regions of high interest, improving coverage efficiency, synthesizing effective control policies for individual agents, and optimizing resource use in settings with unknown prior distributions. We validate our approach through simulations, demonstrating its effectiveness in enhancing coverage and resource allocation.

AI.tldr: Proposes an adaptive coverage strategy that enhances ergodic trajectory optimization with real-time environmental feedback for multi-robot teams, improving coverage efficiency under unknown prior distributions.
AI.motivation: Traditional ergodic search methods optimize robot trajectories but fail to account for unknown prior distributions of the environment, limiting adaptive coverage performance.
AI.method: Enhances ergodic trajectory optimization by constructing a target spatial information distribution from parametric environmental models that are updated online using real-time feedback.
AI.result: Simulation results demonstrate improved coverage efficiency and resource allocation compared to traditional methods.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive coverage framework enables robots to dynamically prioritize high-interest regions and optimize resource use in unknown environments.

title: Investigating Concept Alignment Using Implausible Category Members
summary: Developing AI systems with a human-like understanding of everyday concepts is a key step towards developing safe, reliable systems whose behavior makes sense to humans. When probing concept understanding, asking questions about plausible category members (e.g., "Is a car a vehicle?") is likely to recall patterns in the model's vast training data. We pursue an alternative strategy, characterizing the boundaries of conceptual categories by asking about implausible category members (e.g., "Is an olive a vehicle?") to probe the kind of concept-level knowledge we take for granted in fellow humans. We characterize concept boundaries for a set of fundamental concepts by studying AI systems' assignments of objects to superordinate categories from a classic psychological study by Rosch and Mervis, as well as their assignments of the same objects to mismatched superordinate categories. We compare these assignments to those made by human participants on the full range of within-category and cross-category assignment tasks. Our results reveal a range of concepts for which which models differ in meaningful and surprising ways from humans, including treating "words" as belonging to categories like "vehicles" and "clothing," identifying several "vegetable" category members as "fruit," and assigning exemplars from non-weapon categories to the "weapons" category. We also demonstrate how these instances of concept misalignment translate into problematic downstream behavior with implications for AI safety.

AI.tldr: This study probes AI concept understanding by testing boundaries of categories using implausible members (e.g., "Is an olive a vehicle?"), finding that AI systems differ meaningfully from humans in concept boundaries, including misclassifying "words" as vehicles/clothing, vegetables as fruit, and non-weapons as weapons, with downstream safety implications.
AI.motivation: Developing AI systems with human-like understanding of everyday concepts is crucial for safety and reliability; standard probing with plausible category members may just recall training data patterns, so alternative methods to assess true conceptual understanding are needed.
AI.method: The authors studied AI systems' assignments of objects to superordinate categories from Rosch and Mervis's classic psychology study, including both within-category and mismatched cross-category assignments, then compared results to human judgments.
AI.result: AI models showed meaningful and surprising differences from humans: treating "words" as belonging to "vehicles" and "clothing," identifying some "vegetable" members as "fruit," and assigning non-weapon exemplars to the "weapons" category.
AI.conclusion: Concept misalignment between AI and humans exists in meaningful ways, and these misalignments translate into problematic downstream behavior with implications for AI safety.

title: BEiTScore: Reference-free Image Captioning Evaluation with an Efficient Cross-Encoder Model
summary: Image captioning evaluation remains a significant challenge, as vision-language models evolve toward more challenging capabilities such as generating long-form and context-rich descriptions. State-of-the-art evaluation metrics involve extensive computational costs associated with the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) as judges, or instead suffer from the limitations of standard CLIP-based encoders, such as strict token limits, lack of fine-grained sensitivity, or lack of compositional generalization by treating captions as ``bags-of-words.'' We propose a new learned metric that tackles the aforementioned challenges, based on a lightweight cross-encoder that is initialized from a visual question-answering model checkpoint, balancing a strong weight initialization with computational efficiency. Our training scheme uses a carefully assembled data mixture for supervised learning, featuring adversarial LLM-based data augmentations to enhance model sensitivity to fine-grained visual-linguistic errors. We also introduce a new benchmark designed to assess detailed captioning evaluation across diverse scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed metric achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining the efficiency required for large-scale benchmarking, quality-aware decoding, or reward guidance.

AI.tldr: Introduces a lightweight cross-encoder metric for image captioning evaluation that uses VQA model initialization and adversarial LLM-based data augmentations, achieving SOTA performance with computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation metrics either rely on expensive LLM judges or suffer from CLIP-based encoder limitations (token limits, lack of fine-grained sensitivity, compositional generalization issues).
AI.method: Lightweight cross-encoder initialized from a VQA model checkpoint; supervised learning with adversarial LLM-based data augmentations; a new benchmark for detailed captioning evaluation.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance while maintaining efficiency suitable for large-scale benchmarking, quality-aware decoding, or reward guidance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed metric balances strong evaluation capability with computational practicality, addressing key limitations of existing metrics.

title: Learning Altruistic Collaboration in Heterogeneous Multi-Team Systems
summary: This paper studies heterogeneous multi-team collaboration through dynamic robot allocation, where robots are treated as transferable resources. Leveraging Hamilton's rule from ecology as an altruistic decision-making mechanism, we propose a multi-team collaborative resource allocation framework with heterogeneous capabilities, transfer costs, and capability-dependent contributions. The resulting allocation problem is combinatorial and is shown to be NP-hard. To address scalability, we develop a graph neural network policy under centralized training and decentralized execution that approximates the altruistic allocations based on Hamilton's rule. The model operates over the team interaction graph and predicts robot-level transfer decisions and next robot-to-team assignments. The proposed approach is validated in a firefighting scenario through simulations and experiments, demonstrating that the learned policy achieves near-optimal performance while scaling to larger systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a graph neural network-based framework using Hamilton's rule for dynamic robot allocation across heterogeneous multi-teams, validated in firefighting scenarios with near-optimal performance and scalability.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of heterogeneous multi-team collaboration through dynamic robot allocation, treating robots as transferable resources across teams.
AI.method: Leverages Hamilton's rule from ecology as an altruistic decision-making mechanism; formulates the allocation problem as NP-hard combinatorial optimization; develops a graph neural network policy with centralized training and decentralized execution to approximate altruistic allocations.
AI.result: The learned policy achieves near-optimal performance and scales effectively to larger systems in firefighting simulations and experiments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GNN-based framework effectively enables scalable, near-optimal heterogeneous multi-team robot allocation inspired by ecological altruism principles.

title: The Impact of AI Usage and Informativeness on Skill Development in Logical Reasoning
comment: Accepted at Hybrid Human Artificial Intelligence (HHAI) 2026
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) is being increasingly integrated into human problem-solving, yet its effects on individual skill development remain unclear. We examine how both AI usage and informativeness can shape learning in the context of a controlled logical reasoning task with on-demand access to AI assistance. We find that greater AI usage is associated with weaker skill development: heavy AI users underperform relative to comparable peers, whereas light AI users perform similarly to matched users who do not use AI. We also find in our study that these patterns are mediated by AI informativeness. Low-information AI neither improves immediate performance nor preserves performance after AI assistance is removed, and is linked to weaker learning overall. On the other hand, high-information AI was found to improve short-run performance without reducing post-AI outcomes on average in our experiments, but with heterogeneous effects. Our findings in general suggest that AI can, depending on context, either complement human skill development by amplifying independent reasoning or can act as a substitute that undermines such reasoning, with the implication that regulating AI access and usage will be important for promoting skill development in the presence of AI assistance.

AI.tldr: AI usage in problem-solving tasks can either complement or undermine skill development depending on AI informativeness; high-information AI helps short-term performance without harming learning, while low-information AI and heavy usage weaken skill development.
AI.motivation: To understand how AI integration into problem-solving affects individual skill development, given the unclear effects of AI on learning.
AI.method: Controlled logical reasoning task experiment with on-demand AI assistance, comparing AI usage patterns and informativeness levels.
AI.result: Heavy AI users underperform vs. peers; light users perform similarly to non-users. Low-information AI harms learning; high-information AI improves short-run performance without reducing post-AI outcomes on average, but with heterogeneous effects.
AI.conclusion: AI can complement or substitute human skill development depending on context (informativeness and usage patterns), suggesting regulation of AI access/usage is important for promoting learning.

title: Improving 3D Labeling in Self-Driving by Inferring Vehicle Information using Vision Language Models
comment: To appear in Proceedings of the IEEE Intelligent Vehicles Symposium (IV), 2026. Accepted for oral presentation
summary: We present an approach to improve 3D vehicle labeling in self-driving applications through zero-shot inference of vehicle information, leveraging Vehicle Make and Model Recognition (VMMR) methods. The proposed approach utilizes a Vision Language Model (VLM) to both infer a vehicle's make, model, and generation from image crops, and output accurate 3D bounding box dimensions to seed manual labeling. We evaluate the impact of iterative prompt engineering and the choice of different VLMs on both vehicle bounding box inference and make/model/generation recognition. When compared to strong baselines, the proposed approach not only shows high accuracy, but also excels in mitigating specific failure modes where VLMs provide better dimensions than initial lidar-aided human annotated labels (e.g., in cases of significant vehicle occlusion). Experiments on both public and proprietary data strongly suggest that our conclusions are generalizable across different labelers and datasets. The results demonstrate that integrating VLMs into the labeling process can reduce manual labeling time while increasing label quality.

AI.tldr: This paper uses Vision Language Models (VLMs) to improve 3D vehicle labeling for self-driving applications by inferring vehicle make, model, and generation from image crops, and outputting accurate 3D bounding box dimensions. The approach reduces manual labeling time while improving label quality, especially in cases of significant vehicle occlusion.
AI.motivation: To improve 3D vehicle labeling quality and efficiency in self-driving applications by leveraging VMMR methods and VLMs, particularly addressing failure modes where VLMs provide better dimensions than lidar-aided human annotations (e.g., under occlusion).
AI.method: Uses a Vision Language Model (VLM) to infer vehicle make, model, and generation from image crops, and outputs accurate 3D bounding box dimensions for seeding manual labeling. Evaluates iterative prompt engineering and different VLM choices on both bounding box inference and make/model/generation recognition.
AI.result: High accuracy compared to strong baselines; excels at mitigating specific failure modes (e.g., vehicle occlusion); reduces manual labeling time while increasing label quality; conclusions are generalizable across different labelers and datasets.
AI.conclusion: Integrating VLMs into the labeling process can reduce manual labeling time while increasing label quality in 3D vehicle labeling for self-driving applications.

title: Safe and Steerable Geometric Motion Policies for Robotic Dexterous Manipulation
comment: 24 pages, 10 figures, 5 tables. Project page and demo video: https://tml.stanford.edu/safe-pbds
summary: Robotic dexterous manipulation requires continuously reconciling objectives and constraints defined on heterogeneous geometric spaces: a robot controlled on a $\mathbb{R}^7$ configuration manifold may need to track end effector poses on $\mathrm{SE}(3)$ while satisfying obstacle avoidance margins in $\mathbb{R}$. We present Safe Pullback Bundle Dynamical Systems (SafePBDS), a geometrically consistent framework that computes optimal, certifiably safe configuration manifold accelerations from objectives and safety requirements on arbitrary task manifolds. SafePBDS builds on prior work that combines predefined task manifold dynamical systems to produce autonomous motion. Its first innovation is a pullback control barrier function construction, which converts task manifold safety conditions into linear constraints on configuration manifold accelerations. The second innovation is a task manifold action interface that allows a high-level policy to inject low dimensional residual motions; zero input recovers the autonomous behavior, while safety is preserved under arbitrary inputs. This lets high-level policies efficiently steer exploration while leaving precise motion to the autonomous behavior. We validate SafePBDS in simulation and on a 23-DOF Franka Panda-Allegro Hand platform. On dexterous grasping, SafePBDS achieves a $92.5\%$ success rate across 20 household objects and 120 trials. Using the action interface, the method can exclude any one of the four fingers during grasping via a one-dimensional action, achieving $94.4\%$ 3-finger grasp success across 3 objects and 36 trials. The efficient planning and safety guarantee of SafePBDS also enables the first model-based, fully actuated palm-down in-hand reorientation, exceeding $360^\circ$ of yaw rotation in both directions under varying object weight and wrist motion. Demo video and details: https://tml.stanford.edu/safe-pbds

AI.tldr: SafePBDS is a geometrically consistent framework that computes optimal, certifiably safe configuration manifold accelerations from objectives and safety requirements on arbitrary task manifolds. It achieves 92.5% grasping success and enables the first model-based palm-down in-hand reorientation exceeding 360°.
AI.motivation: Robotic dexterous manipulation requires reconciling objectives and constraints defined on heterogeneous geometric spaces (e.g., ℝ⁷ configuration manifold, SE(3) poses, ℝ safety margins), which existing methods struggle to handle in a geometrically consistent and certifiably safe manner.
AI.method: SafePBDS introduces two key innovations: (1) a pullback control barrier function that converts task manifold safety conditions into linear constraints on configuration manifold accelerations, and (2) a task manifold action interface that allows high-level policies to inject low-dimensional residual motions while preserving safety.
AI.result: 92.5% grasping success across 20 household objects and 120 trials; 94.4% 3-finger grasp success via 1D action interface; first model-based palm-down in-hand reorientation exceeding 360° yaw rotation under varying conditions.
AI.conclusion: SafePBDS provides a geometrically consistent safety-guaranteed framework that enables efficient planning and certification, demonstrated through high grasping success rates and novel in-hand reorientation capabilities.

title: Latent-space Attacks for Refusal Evasion in Language Models
summary: Safety-aligned language models are trained to refuse harmful requests, yet refusal behavior can be suppressed by steering their internal representations. Existing methods do so by ablating a refusal direction from model activations, aiming to remove refusal from the model's residual stream. Despite their empirical success, these methods lack a principled account of the latent-space transformation they induce and why it suppresses refusal. In this work, we recast refusal suppression as a latent-space evasion attack against linear probes trained to separate refused from answered prompts. Under this view, prior work's difference-in-means direction naturally defines such a probe, and its ablation is exactly a projection onto its decision boundary, i.e., a minimum-confidence evasion attack. This perspective not only explains the empirical success of prior work but also admits a key limitation: evasion stops at the decision boundary, motivating the need to push representations further into the compliant region, i.e., where the model answers. We leverage this by proposing a Controlled Latent-space Evasion attack that projects representations past the boundary with an optimized confidence. We achieve state-of-the-art attack success rate across 15 instruction-tuned, multimodal, and reasoning models, outperforming existing refusal-ablation baselines and specialized jailbreak attacks.

AI.tldr: Existing refusal suppression methods ablate a refusal direction from model activations without principled explanation; this paper reframes it as latent-space evasion attack against linear probes, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Existing refusal suppression methods lack a principled account of why they work; the authors aim to provide theoretical grounding and identify limitations of current approaches.
AI.method: Recasts refusal suppression as latent-space evasion attack; proposes Controlled Latent-space Evasion (CLE) attack that projects representations past the decision boundary with optimized confidence.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art attack success rate across 15 instruction-tuned, multimodal, and reasoning models, outperforming existing refusal-ablation baselines and specialized jailbreak attacks.
AI.conclusion: The latent-space evasion perspective explains prior work's success, reveals its limitation (stopping at decision boundary), and motivates pushing representations into the compliant region for better attack performance.

title: BodyReLux: Temporally Consistent Full-Body Video Relighting
comment: Siggraph 2026 Journal Track. Project page: https://eyeline-labs.github.io/bodyrelux/
summary: Being able to relight human performance is a fundamental task for post production and content creation. We present BodyReLux, a subject-specific video diffusion-based framework for relighting full-body human performances in a temporally consistent way. Our model is trained on a hybrid dataset of pixel-aligned video relighting pairs, covering a diverse combination of lighting conditions, performances and viewpoints. To acquire such dataset, we combine traditional static One-Light-at-a-Time (OLAT) capture and a novel dynamic performance capture in which two smoothly varying lighting sequences are rapidly interleaved. Because the lighting operates above the human flicker-fusion threshold, the interleaving does not appear to strobe. We train our video relighting model from a pretrained text-to-video model to fully leverage the generative priors for producing high quality videos. To achieve accurate lighting control, we introduce a new lighting conditioning method that represents each light source as a token. We further condition on sequences of lighting using masked attention to support dynamic lighting control. Together with a carefully designed data augmentation pipeline, we achieve photorealistic, robust, and temporally consistent video relighting of subject-specific human performances.

AI.tldr: BodyReLux is a video diffusion framework for temporally consistent relighting of full-body human performances, trained on a hybrid dataset combining static OLAT captures and novel interleaved dynamic captures, using lighting token conditioning and masked attention for accurate control.
AI.motivation: Relighting human performances is a fundamental task for post-production and content creation, but existing methods lack temporal consistency and robustness for dynamic full-body performances.
AI.method: 1) Hybrid dataset: combines static OLAT captures with novel interleaved dynamic captures (two flicker-free lighting sequences alternating above human perception threshold). 2) Video diffusion model fine-tuned from pretrained text-to-video model. 3) Lighting conditioning via token representation per light source. 4) Masked attention for sequential lighting control. 5) Data augmentation pipeline for robustness.
AI.result: Achieves photorealistic, robust, and temporally consistent video relighting of subject-specific human performances.
AI.conclusion: BodyReLux demonstrates that combining hybrid capture data with generative video diffusion priors and token-based lighting conditioning enables high-quality, temporally coherent human relighting.

title: Analytical and Experimental Force Analysis of a Soft Linear Pneumatic Actuator
summary: Soft sleeve actuators (SSAs) have recently been developed as a pneumatic actuation approach for wearable and assistive robotic systems. By integrating the actuation structure into a sleeve-like geometry, these actuators can reduce reliance on external attachment layers and transmission mechanisms while maintaining compliance with limb-shaped surfaces. However, the force-generation behavior of SSAs remains insufficiently explained, particularly with respect to the variation of output force during extension, the influence of external loading, and the mechanical role of axial stiffness. This paper presents an analytical and experimental force analysis of a linear soft sleeve actuator (LSSA). A quasi-static analytical model was developed by expressing the net axial force as the pressure-generated contribution from the cap and folded walls, reduced by the force associated with axial stiffness. The model incorporates internal pressure, projected pressure areas, folded wall geometry, axial displacement, and an experimentally fitted axial stiffness relation. Prescribed-extension and static-load experiments were conducted to evaluate the actuator response. At 125 kPa, the generated force decreased from approximately 112 N at zero extension to nearly zero at 40 mm. Static loading delayed measurable force generation and reduced force output, particularly at low and intermediate pressures. The results show that LSSA force generation is governed by coupled effects of pressure, geometry, displacement, loading, and axial stiffness.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the force-generation behavior of linear soft sleeve actuators (LSSAs) through analytical modeling and experiments, showing that output force decreases with extension (from ~112 N at 0 mm to ~0 N at 40 mm at 125 kPa) and is influenced by pressure, geometry, displacement, loading, and axial stiffness.
AI.motivation: Soft sleeve actuators (SSAs) reduce reliance on external attachment layers and transmission mechanisms, but their force-generation behavior remains insufficiently explained, particularly regarding output force variation during extension, external loading effects, and the mechanical role of axial stiffness.
AI.method: A quasi-static analytical model was developed expressing net axial force as pressure-generated contributions from the cap and folded walls minus the force associated with axial stiffness, incorporating internal pressure, projected pressure areas, folded wall geometry, axial displacement, and an experimentally fitted axial stiffness relation. Prescribed-extension and static-load experiments were conducted.
